The Nasdaq-100 is set to become even more volatile as SpaceX joins the index before Tuesday's market open, adding a stock known for large price swings. Because SpaceX will not be eligible for inclusion in the S&P 500 for at least another year, the move is expected to further widen the volatility gap between the two benchmark indexes.  The Cboe Nasdaq-100 Volatility Index (VXN) has surged about 43% this year through Thursday, compared with an 8% increase in the Cboe Volatility Index (VIX), highlighting much higher expected volatility for the Nasdaq-100 than for the S&P 500. Analysts say the addition of SpaceX could amplify that divergence given the company's outsized market moves and growing influence within the technology sector.  The Nasdaq-100 already has a heavier concentration in high-growth technology stocks than the S&P 500, making it more sensitive to swings in large-cap names.   $SPCX  $IWB  $ALAB $ARM  $VXN

$ARM  Arm Holdings is basically sitting at the center of the AI data center stack right now.    They don’t manufacture chips, they license the architecture. That model is already embedded across Oracle, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Meta. So the footprint is there, it’s just not always visible on the surface. $ARM   The “ARM is overvalued” argument ignores why the market is willing to pay a premium.  ARM isn’t just another semiconductor company. It licenses the architecture that powers billions of devices worldwide and collects royalties as customers ship more chips. That’s an asset-light, high-margin business model with enormous operating leverage.  The next leg of growth isn’t just smartphones: • AI PCs • AI smartphones • Cloud CPUs • Automotive • Edge AI • IoT • Data centers  Every one of those markets is expanding, and ARM sits at the center of the ecosystem.  Bears love pointing to the P/E. That’s what people also did with Nvidia years ago. Great companies rarely look “cheap” using backward-looking metrics because the market prices future cash flows, not yesterday’s earnings.  If ARM continues increasing royalty rates, expanding into servers, and benefiting from the AI buildout, today’s valuation could look inexpensive in hindsight.  A premium valuation isn’t the same as overvaluation. Dominant platforms with massive moats, recurring royalty streams, and decades-long growth runways deserve premium multiples. $ARM $NVDA $INTC $AVGO $MU  Traditionally, Arm only designed chip blueprints and licensed them to other companies like Apple, Qualcomm, and Samsung. However, the company recently broke its 35-year tradition by releasing its own physical, in-house processor—the Arm AGI CPU—built for artificial intelligence workloads in data centers.The Business BreakdownThe Core Business (Licensing): Arm's primary model remains designing processor architecture and selling those blueprints to other semiconductor manufacturers. These companies take Arm's designs and customize them into the chips used in billions of smartphones, PCs, and smart devices worldwide.The New Chipmaker Role: Arm has entered the physical silicon market to produce its own CPUs. The company's new AGI CPU is focused on high-efficiency power usage for data centers and AI. Rather than operating its own fabrication plants (fabs), Arm outsources the actual manufacturing of these chips to major foundries like TSMC.Customers & Competitors: Major tech giants, including Meta, OpenAI, and Cloudflare, are the first customers for Arm's new physical chips. This move puts Arm in direct competition with traditional data center chipmakers like Intel and AMD.

$POET Could POET become the $ARM  of photonics?    I'm not comparing the technology. CPUs and photonics are completely different. I'm talking about the business model.    $ARM  became incredibly valuable because it doesn't manufacture processors. It provides the architecture and IP that companies like Apple, Qualcomm, and MediaTek use to build their own chips.  Everyone creates their own products, but they all rely on ARM's platform.    What if $POET  could do something similar in photonics?  Instead of trying to make the best laser or the best photodiode, POET's real value could be its Optical Interposer. Imagine a future where:  One company provides the laser.  Another provides the modulator.  Another provides the photodiode.  Another provides the electronics.  And they all integrate their components on POET's Optical Interposer.    That would save time, reduce costs, simplify manufacturing, and allow companies to mix and match the best components without redesigning everything.

UBS raised its price targets on AMD and Arm, citing both companies as key beneficiaries of growing demand for standalone CPUs in agentic AI infrastructure. Analyst Timothy Arcuri increased his target for AMD to $670 from $455 and for Arm to $470 from $260, while maintaining valuation multiples of 35x and 108x, respectively.  The firm pushed back against some of the market’s most aggressive CPU market forecasts, arguing that DRAM supply constraints are likely to limit the total addressable market before 2030. For AMD, UBS sees a competitive advantage in standalone agentic AI racks, which require greater core density and performance.
